text: In an intrinsic semiconductor at room temperature, the number of holes equals:
options: Number of free electrons (\(n_e = n_h = n_i\))
explanation: Intrinsic semiconductor: thermal excitation creates electron-hole pairs. \(n_e = n_h = n_i\) (intrinsic carrier concentration). At 0 K: perfect insulator.
name: Intrinsic Semiconductor

text: The mass action law for semiconductors states:
options: \( n_e \cdot n_h = n_i^2 \) (constant at given temperature)
explanation: Mass action law: \( n_e n_h = n_i^2 \). Doping increases one carrier type but decreases the other. Product remains constant at given T.
name: Mass Action Law

text: In a p-n junction, the depletion region is formed by:
options: Diffusion of majority carriers across junction, leaving ionised atoms
explanation: Depletion region: electrons from n-side diffuse to p-side, holes from p-side to n-side. Ionised donors (n-side) and acceptors (p-side) remain → built-in potential barrier.
name: Depletion Region Formation

text: A p-n junction diode conducts in:
options: Forward bias (forward current flows when V > knee voltage ~0.7V for Si)
explanation: Forward bias: p-side to + terminal. Reduces barrier → significant current flows. Reverse bias: increases barrier → only tiny leakage current.
name: Diode Conduction

text: The knee voltage (cut-in voltage) for a silicon p-n junction diode is approximately:
explanation: Si diode: knee voltage ≈ 0.7 V. Ge diode: ≈ 0.3 V. Below knee voltage, current is negligible. Si diodes are more common (higher temperature stability).
name: Knee Voltage of Si Diode

text: In a half-wave rectifier, the output frequency is:
explanation: Half-wave rectifier: conducts only during positive half-cycle. Output frequency = input frequency. Full-wave: output frequency = 2f.
name: Half-Wave Rectifier Frequency

options: Voltage regulator (operated in reverse breakdown region)
explanation: Zener diode: designed to operate in reverse breakdown at a fixed Zener voltage \(V_Z\). Output voltage stays constant despite input variation → voltage regulation.
name: Zener Diode Application

text: The transistor in common emitter configuration has:
explanation: CE configuration: \( \beta = I_C/I_B \gg 1 \). Both current gain and voltage gain > 1 → power amplification. Most used transistor configuration.
name: Common Emitter Current Gain

text: The current gain \(\alpha\) in common base configuration is:
explanation: CB configuration: \( \alpha = I_C/I_E < 1 \). Relation: \( \beta = \alpha/(1-\alpha) \) or \( \alpha = \beta/(1+\beta) \). \( I_E = I_C + I_B \).
name: Common Base Current Gain

text: In an n-type semiconductor, the majority carriers are:
options: Free electrons (from pentavalent donor impurity atoms)
explanation: n-type: doped with Group 15 (pentavalent) elements (P, As, Sb). Extra electron per dopant → electrons are majority carriers. Holes are minority carriers.
name: n-type Majority Carriers

text: The output of a NAND gate is:
options: 0 when both inputs are 1, 1 otherwise (complement of AND)
explanation: NAND = NOT AND. Output: 0 only when both inputs are 1; otherwise output is 1. Truth table: 11→0, 10→1, 01→1, 00→1. Universal gate.
name: NAND Gate Output

text: The truth table of an OR gate: output is 1 when:
explanation: OR gate: output = 1 if any input = 1. Output = 0 only when ALL inputs = 0. Boolean: \( Y = A+B \) (logical sum).
name: OR Gate Truth Table

text: An LED (Light Emitting Diode) emits light due to:
options: Recombination of electrons and holes releasing photons (electroluminescence)
explanation: LED: electron-hole recombination in forward-biased p-n junction releases energy as photon. Color depends on bandgap energy. More efficient than incandescent bulbs.
name: LED Working Principle

text: The forbidden energy gap (band gap) of silicon is approximately:
explanation: Band gaps: Ge ≈ 0.67 eV, Si ≈ 1.1 eV, GaAs ≈ 1.43 eV, ZnSe ≈ 3.2 eV. Conductors: 0 eV. Insulators: > 5 eV.
name: Band Gap of Silicon

text: A transistor can be used as a switch because:
options: In saturation region it is ON (low resistance), in cut-off region it is OFF (high resistance)
explanation: Transistor switch: Saturation (base driven hard) = ON state. Cut-off (base current = 0) = OFF state. Used in digital logic circuits and power electronics.
name: Transistor as Switch
